title: Traction Splint
content_markdown:
#### Indications
- Patient with a suspected femur fracture.

#### Procedure

- Explain to the patient that this procedure requires straps to be applied in the groin area.

- Administer pain relief prior to moving injured leg / applying traction splint.

- Remove footwear/socks, belts, and any objects from pockets.

- Align, support, and apply manual traction to injured leg.

- Check distal pulses, sensation, and capillary refill.

- Position the splint alongside uninjured limb and adjust splint to 15cm beyond patient’s foot with the heel stand flat.

- Position Velcro straps so that two are above and two are below the knee.

- Reposition splint alongside injured limb.

- Apply the ankle strap, grasp ring and maintain manual traction.

- Whilst supporting the patient’s injured limb, elevate and slide the splint underneath. Position the ischial ring in the crease of the buttock. Fold heel stand down.

- Secure ischial ring strap.

- Attach ankle strap to “S” hook and tension until straps are firm and adequate traction or patient comfort has been achieved.

- Secure the four Velcro straps. Ensure the straps are not over the fracture site.

- Continue to assess the limb for colour, temperature and perfusion.

- Administer further analgesia as appropriate.

#### Contraindications
- Lower leg or multiple leg fractures.
- Neck of femur fracture.

#### Complications
- In the setting of a suspected pelvic fracture consider which is the most critical injury.
- If a pelvic and traction splint are both indicated consider the impact of pressure on the pelvis from use of a traction splint: 
- Consider anatomical splinting as an alternative.
- Consider manual traction.
- If a traction splint and pelvic splint are both applied: 
- The groin strap may need to be applied under the pelvic splint.
- The pelvic splint should be tightened prior to traction.
- If pain increases or haemodynamic status deteriorates the traction splint should be removed and manual techniques applied.
- Splinting should not delay extrication and transport of the critical patient.
